Analyze the directional influence between variables using information theory metrics.
Select variables to analyze for directional relationships
Compares uncertainty reduction in both directions
Normalized information gain in both directions
Direct measure of causal influence direction
Chi-square is recommended for most cases
Higher values give more accurate p-values but take longer

Directional analysis uses information theory to infer the likely direction of influence between variables. Unlike correlation, which only measures the strength of association, directional analysis can suggest which variable is more likely to influence the other.
Measures the amount of information shared between variables. Higher values indicate stronger association.
Compares H(Y|X) with H(X|Y). If H(Y|X) < H(X|Y), X likely influences Y.
Normalizes information gain by the entropy of each variable.
A single metric that ranges from -1 to 1. Positive values suggest X influences Y, negative values suggest Y influences X.
While these metrics can suggest the direction of influence, they cannot prove causality. Validation through controlled experiments or domain knowledge is still recommended.

When analyzing variables with significantly different entropy levels (e.g., binary gender vs. continuous income), directional analysis can be biased. Variables with lower entropy tend to appear as "effect" rather than "cause" variables.

This analysis includes entropy-aware directionality metrics that adjust for these imbalances. When you see a warning symbol (⚠) in the results, pay attention to the "Entropy-Aware" column for a potentially more accurate directionality assessment.
Fast approximation, good for large samples
More accurate for small samples and skewed distributions
Most accurate but slowest, uses randomization testing
